description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, making it easier to contextualize each subsystem (planning, memory, tools) as part of a coherent whole.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall framework; return to it as a reference when introducing each new component.
suggested_caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview with planning, memory, and tools. (Weng, 2023)

description: Side-by-side comparison of reasoning trajectories for ReAct vs. baselines across four tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER, AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought/Action/Observation steps versus Act-only traces.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how ReAct's interleaved reasoning and acting differs from act-only approaches, letting students trace exactly where reasoning traces add value and where act-only methods fail.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use it to walk students through a specific trajectory step-by-step to contrast Thought/Action/Observation with Act-only behavior.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories vs. Act-only baseline across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart of the Reflexion framework showing the cycle: Actor generates a trajectory, Evaluator scores it, Self-Reflection module produces verbal feedback, which is stored in episodic memory and fed back to the Actor for the next trial.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the feedback loop that enables iterative self-improvement in agents, making the abstract concept of 'dynamic memory and self-reflection' concrete and traceable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Reflexion after ReAct; use the diagram to trace one full cycle (trajectory → evaluation → reflection → memory → next trial) before discussing experimental results.
suggested_caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: Actor, Evaluator, and episodic memory. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA tasks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and quantifies the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. inefficient planning as failure modes, grounding theoretical claims in data.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework diagram; use it to ask students why hallucination dominates over inefficient planning in AlfWorld and what that implies about agent design.
suggested_caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning in AlfWorld. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, etc.) is fed as a prefix, and the model is trained to predict only the final best output.
teaching_value: Makes the CoH training objective tangible by showing the ranked sequence structure, helping students understand how conditioning on past feedback enables the model to learn from its own mistakes.
when_to_show: Introduce when teaching self-improvement via fine-tuning; contrast with RLHF to highlight how CoH uses explicit sequential feedback rather than reward signals.
suggested_caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns to improve outputs conditioned on ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): multi-episode learning histories from source RL policies are concatenated and used to train a transformer via behavioral cloning, enabling in-context reinforcement learning.
teaching_value: Clarifies the non-obvious mechanism by which a neural network can learn an RL algorithm itself (rather than a policy) by showing how cross-episode history is structured as training data.
when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL; show the diagram before discussing why context window length matters, then connect to the finding that 2–4 episodes are needed for near-optimal performance.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a transformer via behavioral cloning. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing learning curves across episodes.
teaching_value: Empirically validates that AD achieves near-source-policy performance and outperforms ED, demonstrating that distilling the learning algorithm (not just the final policy) is beneficial.
when_to_show: Show after the AD mechanism diagram; prompt students to identify which baseline AD most closely matches and discuss what the gap between AD and source policy implies.
suggested_caption: AD matches source policy performance on memory/exploration tasks, outperforming ED and RL². (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory types.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping biological memory categories to LLM agent memory implementations (e.g., in-context storage, external vector databases), helping students understand design choices through analogy.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module; use the human memory analogy to motivate why agents need different memory types before introducing technical implementations like vector stores.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, ScaNN, FAISS variants) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.
teaching_value: Quantifies the accuracy-speed tradeoff inherent in ANN algorithms for vector retrieval, helping students understand why approximate methods are chosen over exact search in production agent memory systems.
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing external memory retrieval; use the chart to ask students why no single algorithm dominates and what factors (latency vs. recall) would drive a real system design choice.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and multi-model coordination in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, call execution, and result evaluation across three levels of tool-use difficulty.
teaching_value: Breaks down the abstract concept of 'tool use' into discrete, evaluable decision points (which API to call, how to parameterize it, whether the result is correct), helping students understand how agent tool-use capability can be benchmarked systematically.
when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use decisions are structured and evaluated; use it when discussing how we measure agent accuracy at each step of an API interaction.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.
teaching_value: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning modules combine to produce believable long-term agent behavior, giving students a concrete architectural model for understanding how agents maintain context and simulate human-like cognition over time.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use to agent simulation topics, or when teaching memory and planning as agent components; use it to ground the abstract idea of 'believable human behavior' in a concrete architectural design.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable human-like behavior. (Park et al. 2023)

description: A LangGraph control-flow diagram illustrating deferred node execution: a node returns a Command that routes execution to a later node instead of immediately running the next step, enabling custom retry/loop behavior.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize how LangGraph can pause or redirect execution at runtime, clarifying how to implement retries, recursion limits, or stepwise workflows by returning Commands rather than hard-wiring edges.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how to delay a node, implement retries/backoff, or dynamically choose the next node during execution.
suggested_caption: Deferred node execution via Command routing (LangGraph docs).

description: A map-reduce style graph showing fan-out from a single node into multiple parallel map tasks and a fan-in reduce/aggregate step, using the send API to dispatch work items.
teaching_value: Makes the fan-out/fan-in pattern concrete so students can map it to parallel processing: sending multiple messages/tasks, then aggregating results deterministically in a reducer node.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to process a list of inputs concurrently and then combine outputs (e.g., chunked summarization, batch tool calls).
suggested_caption: Map-reduce fanout using the send API (LangGraph docs).

description: A simple loop graph where execution cycles between nodes until a condition is met, then exits to an end state.
teaching_value: Provides an at-a-glance mental model for iterative agent/workflow behavior, helping students understand how LangGraph represents loops as explicit edges and termination conditions.
when_to_show: Show when a student needs to implement repeated steps (e.g., refine-answer loops) and asks how to stop the loop safely.
suggested_caption: Simple loop structure in a LangGraph workflow (LangGraph docs).

description: A more complex looping graph with branching paths inside the loop and an imposed recursion limit to prevent infinite execution.
teaching_value: Helps learners see how branching interacts with loops and why recursion limits matter, reinforcing safe-guarding long-running agents and returning state when limits are hit.
when_to_show: Show when discussing safety controls for agent loops, debugging runaway graphs, or explaining how to handle recursion-limit termination gracefully.
suggested_caption: Branching loop with a recursion limit safeguard (LangGraph docs).

description: A diagram demonstrating Command-based graph navigation where a node both updates state and directs the next node to execute, combining control flow and state mutation.
teaching_value: Clarifies that a single node can decide the next step while also producing state updates, which is key for dynamic routing, tool selection, and conditional workflows.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how to route to different nodes based on intermediate results while still persisting updates to shared state.
suggested_caption: Command combines routing and state updates (LangGraph docs).

description: A large, fractal-like PNG visualization of a complex LangGraph with many repeated substructures, illustrating how nested/recursive patterns can emerge in graph-based workflows.
teaching_value: Gives students an intuition for scale and complexity in real graphs, helping them reason about modularity, repeated motifs, and why tooling/visualization matters for debugging.
when_to_show: Show when introducing graph visualization tools or when a learner struggles to understand or debug a large multi-branch workflow.
suggested_caption: Large-scale LangGraph visualization (LangGraph docs).

description: Diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph/agent run progresses through steps while periodic checkpoints are written to a persistent store, enabling later recovery and inspection.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize what a checkpoint is (a saved snapshot of state) and why persistence matters for resuming interrupted runs and debugging execution over time.
when_to_show: Show when introducing LangGraph persistence or explaining how runs can resume after failure using stored checkpoints.
suggested_caption: Checkpoint snapshots enable persistent LangGraph runs (LangChain docs).

description: Diagram showing retrieval of state history from persisted checkpoints: multiple past states are listed/queried to inspect how the graph state evolved across steps.
teaching_value: Clarifies that persistence is not only for recovery but also for observability—students can inspect prior states to understand and debug agent behavior.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect prior states (e.g., after a run) to diagnose where outputs or decisions changed.
suggested_caption: Query persisted checkpoints to view state history (LangChain docs).

description: Diagram depicting replay: a previously checkpointed run is re-executed from a selected checkpoint to reproduce behavior or continue execution deterministically from that point.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how replay supports reproducibility and iterative debugging by restarting from a known-good state rather than rerunning from the beginning.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to reproduce an issue or continue a run from mid-execution using stored checkpoints.
suggested_caption: Replay execution starting from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain docs).

description: Diagram showing updating state: an existing persisted checkpoint/state is modified (patched) and then execution continues, reflecting the updated state in subsequent steps.
teaching_value: Teaches that persisted state can be edited to correct inputs, inject fixes, or steer execution—useful for human-in-the-loop workflows and recovery from bad intermediate states.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how to intervene in a run—edit a checkpointed state and then resume to test a fix or alternative path.
suggested_caption: Update a checkpointed state and resume execution (LangChain docs).

description: Duplicate of the checkpoints persistence diagram: execution steps with periodic checkpoint snapshots saved to a persistent store for later recovery/inspection.
teaching_value: Reinforces the core mental model of checkpointing as saved state snapshots enabling persistence and restartability.
when_to_show: Show when summarizing persistence concepts in a chat context or when the learner needs a quick refresher on what checkpoints are.
suggested_caption: Checkpoint snapshots enable persistent LangGraph runs (LangChain docs).

description: Duplicate of the state history diagram: querying and listing prior persisted states across checkpoints to inspect evolution over time.
teaching_value: Supports understanding of state introspection and debugging by reviewing historical snapshots.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to view what happened earlier in a run or compare states across steps.
suggested_caption: Query persisted checkpoints to view state history (LangChain docs).

description: Duplicate of the replay diagram: selecting a checkpoint and replaying execution from that saved state to reproduce or continue a run.
teaching_value: Helps learners connect persistence to reproducible debugging and mid-run continuation.
when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducing bugs or rerunning from a specific point without starting over.
suggested_caption: Replay execution starting from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain docs).

description: Duplicate of the update state diagram: modifying a persisted checkpoint/state and resuming execution so downstream steps reflect the change.
teaching_value: Illustrates practical intervention: correcting or steering a run by editing state rather than rerunning everything.
when_to_show: Show when teaching human-in-the-loop correction, recovery from bad intermediate outputs, or experimenting with alternative state values.
suggested_caption: Update a checkpointed state and resume execution (LangChain docs).
